en.extract:
The scissor-tailed flycatcher (Tyrannus forficatus), known as swallow-tailed flycatcher or scissorstail, is a long-tailed insectivorous bird of the genus Tyrannus, whose members are collectively referred to as kingbirds. It is found in North and Central America, and is Oklahoma's State Bird.


Taxonomy
The scissor-tailed flycatcher was formally described in 1789 by the German naturalist Johann Friedrich Gmelin in his revised and expanded edition of Carl Linnaeus's Systema Naturae. He placed it with the flycatchers in the genus Muscicapa and coined the binomial name Muscicapa forficata. The specific epithet is from Latin forfex, forficis meaning "a pair of scissors". Gmelin based his description on "Le moucherolle à queue fourchue du Mexique" (French: "the Mexican swallow-tailed flycatcher") that had been described in 1778 by the French polymath Comte de Buffon from a specimen from Mexico and illustrated with a hand-coloured engraving by François-Nicolas Martinet. 
The scissor-tailed flycatcher is now one of 13 species placed in the kingbird genus Tyrannus that was introduced in 1799 by Bernard Germain de Lacépède. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised. Within the genus Tyrannus, the scissor-tailed flycatcher is most closely related to the western kingbird (Tyrannus verticalis). 


Hybridization
In eastern Arkansas and western Tennessee, there is a hybrid breeding zone where the scissor-tailed flycatcher and the western kingbird are sympatric and possibly compete for the same niche. Both these species have simultaneously expanded their breeding ranges eastward over the past 50 years.


Distribution and habitat
Their breeding habitat is open shrubby country with scattered trees in the south-central states of Texas, Oklahoma, Kansas, western portions of Louisiana, Arkansas, and Missouri west to far eastern New Mexico and northeastern Mexico. Reported sightings record occasional stray visitors as far north as southern Canada and Upstate New York, as far east as Florida and Georgia, and in the West Indies. They migrate through Texas and eastern Mexico to their winter non-breeding range, from southern Mexico to Panama. Pre-migratory roosts and flocks flying south may contain as many as 1000 birds.


Description
Adult birds have pale gray heads and upper parts, light underparts, salmon-pink flanks and undertail coverts, and dark gray wings. Axillars and patch on underwing coverts are red. Their extremely long, forked tails, which are black on top and white on the underside, are characteristic and unmistakable. At maturity, the male may be up to 15 in (38 cm) in length, while the female's tail is up to 30% shorter. The wingspan is 38.1 cm (15.0 in) and the weight is up to 43 g (1.5 oz). Immature birds are duller in color and have shorter tails. A lot of these birds have been reported to be more than 40 cm (16 in).


Breeding

They build a cup nest in isolated trees or shrubs, sometimes using artificial sites such as telephone poles near towns. The male performs a spectacular aerial display during courtship with his long tail forks streaming out behind him. Both parents feed the young. Like other kingbirds, they are very aggressive in defending their nest. Clutches contain three to six eggs.


Diet
In the summer, scissor-tailed flycatchers feed mainly on insects (grasshoppers, robber-flies, and dragonflies), which they may catch by waiting on a perch and then flying out to catch them in flight (hawking). For additional food in the winter they will also eat some berries.


In culture
The scissor-tailed flycatcher is the state bird of Oklahoma, and is displayed in flight with tail feathers spread on the reverse of the Oklahoma Commemorative Quarter. 
Professional soccer team FC Tulsa features a scissor-tailed flycatcher on their crest. The scissor-tailed flycatcher is also displayed in the background of the current license plate.
The bird is featured on the box of the popular 2019 board game Wingspan.
A scissor-tailed flycatcher named MC Sizzy is featured in the 2015 National Geographic Kids TV show 50 Birds, 50 States.


Gallery


References


External links

"Tyrannus forficatus ". Avibase. 
Scissor-tailed flycatcher - Tyrannus forficatus Archived 2004-10-09 at the Wayback Machine - USGS Patuxent Bird Identification InfoCenter
Stamps for El Salvador, Nicaragua, United States at bird-stamps.org
Scissor-tailed flycatcher photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)
"Scissor-tailed flycatcher media". Internet Bird Collection.
Birds of Oklahoma: Scissor-tailed Flycatcher, Oklahoma's State Bird (with link to image gallery)
Scissor-tailed flycatcher species account at Neotropical Birds (Cornell Lab of Ornithology)
Interactive range map of Tyrannus forficatus at IUCN Red List

The '''scissor-tailed flycatcher''' ('''''Tyrannus forficatus'''''), known as '''swallow-tailed flycatcher''' or '''scissorstail''', is a long-tailed [[Insectivore|insectivorous]] bird of the [[genus]] ''Tyrannus'', whose members are collectively referred to as [[kingbird]]s. It is found in [[North America|North]] and [[Central America]], and is Oklahoma's State Bird.

==Taxonomy==
The scissor-tailed flycatcher was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1789 by the German naturalist [[Johann Friedrich Gmelin]] in his revised and expanded edition of [[Carl Linnaeus]]'s ''[[Systema Naturae]]''. He placed it with the flycatchers in the [[genus]] ''[[Muscicapa]]'' and coined the [[binomial nomenclature|binomial name]] ''Muscicapa forficata''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Gmelin | first=Johann Friedrich | author-link=Johann Friedrich Gmelin| year=1789 | title=Systema naturae per regna tria naturae: secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | edition=13th | volume=1, Part 2 | language=Latin | location=Lipsiae [Leipzig] | publisher=Georg. Emanuel. Beer | page=931 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2656426 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Traylor | editor-first=Melvin A. Jr | author-link=Melvin Alvah Traylor Jr. | year=1979 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=8 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=225 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14501002 }}</ref> The specific epithet is from [[Latin]] ''forfex'', ''forficis'' meaning "a pair of scissors".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=162 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n162/mode/1up }}</ref> Gmelin based his description on "Le moucherolle à queue fourchue du Mexique" ([[French language|French]]: "the [[Mexico|Mexican]] swallow-tailed flycatcher") that had been described in 1778 by the French polymath [[Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|Comte de Buffon]] from a specimen from Mexico and illustrated with a hand-coloured engraving by [[François-Nicolas Martinet]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Buffon | first=Georges-Louis Leclerc de | author-link=Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon | year=1778 | title=Histoire Naturelle des Oiseaux | volume=4 | location=Paris | publisher=De l'Imprimerie Royale | pages=564–565 | chapter=Le moucherolle à queue fourchue du Mexique | language=French | chapter-url=https://gallica.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/bpt6k10697142/f640.item }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | last1=Buffon | first1=Georges-Louis Leclerc de | author1-link=Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon | last2=Martinet | first2=François-Nicolas | author2-link=François-Nicolas Martinet | last3=Daubenton | first3=Edme-Louis  | author3-link=Edme-Louis Daubenton | last4=Daubenton | first4=Louis-Jean-Marie | author4-link=Louis-Jean-Marie Daubenton | year=1765–1783 | chapter=Gobe-mouche à queue fourchue, du Mexique | title=Planches Enluminées D'Histoire Naturelle | volume=7 | location=Paris | publisher=De L'Imprimerie Royale | at=Plate 677 | chapter-url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/35219179  }}</ref> 

The scissor-tailed flycatcher is now one of 13 species placed in the kingbird genus ''[[Tyrannus]]'' that was introduced in 1799 by [[Bernard Germain de Lacépède]].<ref name="ioc">{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=January 2023 | title=Tyrant flycatchers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 13.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/flycatchers/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=20 March 2023 }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name="ioc" /> Within the genus ''Tyrannus'', the scissor-tailed flycatcher is most closely related to the [[western kingbird]] (''Tyrannus verticalis'').<ref name="harvey">{{Cite journal | last1=Harvey | first1=M.G. | display-authors=etal | date=2020 | title=The evolution of a tropical biodiversity hotspot | journal=Science | volume=370 | issue=6522 | pages=1343–1348 | doi=10.1126/science.aaz6970 | pmid=33303617 | bibcode=2020Sci...370.1343H | hdl=10138/329703 | hdl-access=free }} A high resolution version of the phylogenetic tree in Figure 1 is available from the first author's website [https://www.harveybirdlab.org/docs/Harveyetal2020_Fig1_tree_HiRes.pdf here].</ref> 

=== Hybridization ===
In eastern [[Arkansas]] and western [[Tennessee]], there is a hybrid breeding zone where the scissor-tailed flycatcher and the western kingbird are sympatric and possibly compete for the same niche.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Worm, J.R. |author2=Roeder, D.V. |author3=Husak, M.S. |author4=Fluker, B.L. |author5=Boves, T.J. |year=2019 |title=Characterizing patterns of introgressive hybridization between two species of Tyrannus following concurrent range expansion |journal=Ibis |volume=161 |issue=4 |pages=770–780 |doi=10.1111/ibi.12674}}</ref> Both these species have simultaneously expanded their breeding ranges eastward over the past 50 years.

==Distribution and habitat==
Their breeding habitat is open shrubby country with scattered trees in the south-central states of [[Texas]], [[Oklahoma]], [[Kansas]], western portions of [[Louisiana]], Arkansas, and [[Missouri]] west to far eastern [[New Mexico]] and northeastern Mexico. Reported sightings record occasional stray visitors as far north as southern [[Canada]] and [[Upstate New York|Upstate]] [[New York (state)|New York]], as far east as [[Florida]] and [[Georgia (U.S. state)|Georgia]], and in the [[West Indies]]. They [[bird migration|migrate]] through Texas and eastern Mexico to their winter non-breeding range, from southern Mexico to [[Panama]]. Pre-migratory roosts and flocks flying south may contain as many as 1000 birds.<ref name="AAB" />

==Description==
Adult birds have pale gray heads and upper parts, light underparts, salmon-pink flanks and undertail [[coverts]], and dark gray wings. Axillars and patch on underwing coverts are red.<ref name=BOC/> Their extremely long, forked tails, which are black on top and white on the underside, are characteristic and unmistakable. At maturity, the male may be up to {{convert|15|in|cm|abbr=on}} in length, while the female's tail is up to 30% shorter. The wingspan is {{convert|38.1|cm|in|abbr=on}} and the weight is up to {{convert|43|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Sibley/> Immature birds are duller in color and have shorter tails. A lot of these birds have been reported to be more than {{convert|40|cm|in|abbr=on}}.{{Citation needed|reason=Reported where and by whom?|date=July 2014}}

== Breeding ==
{{Easy CSS image crop|Image=Scissor-tailed Flycatcher - Papa - 53078648710.jpg|desired_width=250|align=right|crop_left_perc=25|crop_top_perc=10|crop_right_perc=5|caption=In flight, with prey}}
[[File:Scissor-tailed Flycatcher, Cedar Hill, TX, USA imported from iNaturalist photo 405604736.jpg|thumb|Juveniles, in Texas]]
They build a cup nest in isolated trees or shrubs, sometimes using artificial sites such as [[telephone pole]]s near towns. The male performs a spectacular aerial display during courtship with his long tail forks streaming out behind him. Both parents feed the young. Like other kingbirds, they are very aggressive in defending their [[bird nest|nest]]. Clutches contain three to six [[bird egg|eggs]].

== Diet ==
In the summer, scissor-tailed flycatchers feed mainly on insects ([[grasshopper]]s, [[robber-flies]], and [[dragonflies]]), which they may catch by waiting on a perch and then flying out to catch them in flight ([[Hawking (birds)|hawking]]). For additional food in the winter they will also eat some [[berries]].

== In culture ==
The scissor-tailed flycatcher is the [[List of U.S. state birds|state bird]] of [[Oklahoma]], and is displayed in flight with tail feathers spread on the reverse of the [[50 state quarters|Oklahoma Commemorative Quarter]]. 

Professional soccer team [[FC Tulsa]] features a scissor-tailed flycatcher on their crest. The scissor-tailed flycatcher is also displayed in the background of the current license plate.

The bird is featured on the box of the popular 2019 board game [[Wingspan (board game)|Wingspan]].

A scissor-tailed flycatcher named MC Sizzy is featured in the 2015 [[National Geographic Kids]] TV show [[50 Birds, 50 States.|50 Birds, 50 States]].

zh.extract:
剪尾王霸鹟（學名：Tyrannus forficatus，英語：Scissor-tailed Flycatcher），是雀形目霸鹟科霸鶲屬下的一種鳥類。這種鳥類以昆蟲為主食，是會行遷徙的物種，在春季時前往美國南部的稀樹草原繁殖，並在秋冬時向南至佛羅里達及中美洲過冬。這種鳥類以其相當狹長的剪刀形尾巴著稱，是美國奧克拉荷馬州的州鳥。
這種鳥類主要以黑白色系為主，其頭背部偏灰白色，翅膀較黑，長尾則是黑白交錯，在胸腹部則有一些粉紅色及淡黃色羽毛。在雌鳥及幼鳥中，尾巴則相對較短。繁殖時以一夫一妻制為主，雄鳥會有求偶行為，而雌鳥每次產3—6枚卵，12—14天後孵化，14—17天後離巢。在《國際自然保護聯盟瀕危物種紅色名錄》中，因其分佈廣大且數量仍多，被列為無危物種。


物種發現與分類

該物種在乔治-路易·勒克莱尔·德·布丰的《自然通史》（Histoire Naturelle）就已有描述，當時布豐以「Le moucherolle à queue fourchue du Mexique」（墨西哥燕尾鶲）一名稱呼。後來德國博物學家約翰·弗里德里希·格梅林在其1789年改版的《自然系統》的修正版內引布豐的資料以Muscicapa forficata的名稱命名。
1837年，英國鳥類學家威廉·斯溫森將其移至Milvulus屬下，並在1901年時被更名為Muscivora。該屬在1966年時被建議應歸為當時已經建立的霸鶲屬下做為一個亞屬，但最終於1983年時被認為其特徵並不足以成為一個亞屬，因此被直接歸於霸鶲屬至今。
其屬名Tyrannus來自拉丁語的同樣辭彙，源於希臘語的τῠ́ρᾰννος（轉寫：turannos），即「君主」之意。種小名forficatus來自拉丁語forfex，即為「剪刀」之意。
剪尾王霸鶲是霸鶲屬下的十三個成員之一，與其最為親近的物種為西方美洲王霸鶲。這兩個物種同域分佈，生態地位相似，雖然有未能成功孵化的雜交情形，但也有出現了同時擁有兩者特徵的雜交個體。並也有與庫奇氏王霸鶲雜交的個體。目前這種鳥類沒有亞種變化，是單型種。


形態描述
剪尾王霸鹟的體長平均約35公分，其中延長的尾巴就佔了當中18—21.4公分；體重則約39—43公克。其鳥喙寬平均7.6公釐、深平均6.0公釐、嘴峰長平均约22.4公釐；翼長12.1公分；跗蹠長18.6公釐。
成鳥的頭部為白色，鳥喙黑色。上半身灰色，背面呈淡灰色与黑色，胸腹偏粉红色与白色。翅膀邊緣帶有白色，翼下則有淡黃色羽毛，尾羽黑白交錯並在末端呈黑色，其中最外側的尾羽相當長，成為這種鳥類最明顯的特徵。雌性的身体通常比雄性小，尾巴也较短。幼鳥也同樣有較短的尾巴，並缺乏腹部上的粉紅色區塊。
其剪刀形尾巴及其他特徵使其不難與其他鳴禽區分，但當地的維達雀屬鳥類也同樣擁有長尾的特徵。這種鳥類與叉尾王霸鹟的區分點在後者頭部為黑色；而其幼鳥與西方美洲王霸鶲比較時則差在後者有黃色的腹部跟綠色的背部。


分佈及棲息環境
這種鳥類會在3月中旬至4月初時抵達美国南部繁殖，當中包含德克薩斯、奧克拉荷馬、堪薩斯和內布拉斯加。隨後於秋季的10月到11月時集中向南遷徙，並於冬季时于佛罗里达南部、中美洲、南至巴拿马的開闊、潮濕的地區過冬。當中有一些個體可能會遠至阿拉斯加東南部、加拿大南部和大西洋或太平洋沿岸繁殖。
通常夏季時剪尾王霸鶲會在稀樹草原的環境中繁殖，並可在開闊的鄉村地區、農村、電線上或甚至是都市中看到。其冬季的棲息點包括牧場、農田、半濕潤的灌木地帶或熱帶森林邊緣等地。


習性

剪尾王霸鶲慣於群體行動，有時可見超過200隻的大型群體同時遷徙或過冬。其飛行時尾巴常折疊於身後，翅膀拍動較淺而輕盈，並常常飛行時急轉彎。這種鳥類也被觀察到會在地面上行走及跳躍移動，且曾被觀察到進行蟻浴。
就像其他霸鶲屬鳥類，剪尾王霸鶲也有追趕其他鳥類的習性，曾記錄過的鳥類包含紅尾鵟、斯温氏鵟、紅頭美洲鷲、哀鴿、大尾擬八哥、普通擬八哥、小嘲鶇、西方美洲王霸鶲、呆頭伯勞、家麻雀、短嘴鴉、冠藍鴉及鷚雀鵐，據信是為了驅趕潛在的捕食者及資源的競爭者。


食性
剪尾王霸鶲以在空中捕獲昆蟲為食，主要以蚱蜢跟甲蟲為食，並也會吃蜜蜂、黃蜂、蜘蛛、蟋蟀、水果和漿果。通常這種鳥類會在較高處觀察昆蟲良久才飛起在上方盤旋後才俯下捕捉。少數情況下有可能會在牛背上出現。


繁殖
剪尾王霸鶲視位置會在不同時間開始繁殖，在南部的族群約4月左右開始；而北部則會延遲到6月，採一夫一妻制。在春天時，牠們會用其長尾巴進行求偶表演：雄鳥會自100英尺（30米）的高空俯衝而下，一邊之字型飛行一邊發出叫聲，再飛上空中做出數次後空翻的動作展示其尾羽，重複數次。在繁殖季中，牠們會具有領域性，並驅趕靠近的其他同類。

巢穴會由雌鳥築於5至30英尺（1.5至9.1米）高的樹枝分叉上，以樹枝、根、莖、棉花、纖維、布料製成，呈杯狀。雌鳥通常產3—6枚卵，每隔一天產一枚，並獨自孵化。卵呈卵圓形，為略帶光澤的白色或淺粉色，並可能帶有一些深色斑點或斑塊，約為長22公釐、寬17公釐的大小。其巢穴是褐頭牛鸝的少見寄主，但在研究中發現剪尾王霸鶲有能力自行排除牛鸝的寄生卵。
幼鳥在12—13天的孵化後誕生，並同時由父母照顧餵食（但其中約七成五仍由雌性負責），餵食時間集中於傍晚時段。在孵化14—17天後具有離巢能力。


叫聲
剪尾王霸鶲的叫聲較西方美洲王霸鶲的歌聲和緩且低，且略有不同的節奏和音調。聲音是相對低但仍尖銳的「bik」、「kew」、「ka-quee-ka-quee」或重複的「ka-lup」，通常在停棲或展示時可聽見。黎明時能聽到「pup、pup、pup、perek」的聲音。


威脅
其巢穴有可能遭到當地雷暴或龍捲風影響而大量損失，因此使當年的繁殖成功率大幅下降。而蛇類及庫柏鷹也曾有食用其卵或雛鳥的紀錄。


物種狀態
在《國際自然保護聯盟瀕危物種紅色名錄》中，由於其廣大的分佈範圍及下降速度不大，因此被列入無危物種。目前總體數量在2019年預估有910萬隻，但在2008—2018年間減少了約7％的數量。但因這種鳥類能在在人類干擾下生存，目前並沒有與其相關的保育措施規劃，且其族群有向東擴展的跡象。


文化

在美國奧克拉荷馬州的州鳥是剪尾王霸鶲，這種鳥類也同樣出現在該州的25美分紀念幣的背面也，以及該州的美國足球冠軍聯賽足球隊FC塔爾薩的標誌上。其長尾羽也曾被用來製作為扇子使用。
2019年的德國年度遊戲獎的最佳桌上遊戲《展翅翱翔》的盒裝封面上即繪有一隻剪尾王霸鶲。
